title: 7 Foundational Principles of Data Protection by Design
description: The seven foundational DPbD principles referenced by the JPDP DPbD Guideline (issued 30 April 2026). Originally articulated by Dr. Ann Cavoukian (Information and Privacy Commissioner of Ontario). Provide a guiding framework for operationalising DPbD; not exhaustive.

principles.name: Proactive not reactive; preventative not remedial
principles.summary: Anticipate and prevent privacy risks before they occur; build processes to prevent breaches rather than reacting after the fact.
operationalisation: Senior leadership commitment to a strong proactive privacy programme
Director-level training and personal data protection expertise on the board
Adequate resourcing for the privacy programme
Personal data protection compliance in senior management performance evaluation
Regular audits and DPIAs reported to the board
Culture and environment where stakeholders can suggest improvements
Systematic methods to recognise poor designs and correct negative impacts before they occur

principles.name: Privacy as the default setting
principles.summary: Personal data is automatically protected in any system. Even if the individual does nothing, their privacy remains secure — protection is built in, by default.
operationalisation: Begin with no collection of personal data unless a specific compelling purpose is defined
Purpose specification: communicate purposes at or before collection, clear/limited/relevant
Collection limitation: lawful and limited to what is necessary
Data minimisation: collection kept to a strict minimum; default to non-identifiable interactions
Use, retention, disclosure limitation: only for identified/consented purposes; retain only as long as necessary
Adopt opt-in consent systems
Where need is unclear: presumption of privacy and precautionary principle apply (most privacy-protective default)
Technological/policy/procedural barriers to data linkage; isolate data processed for different purposes

principles.name: Privacy embedded into design
principles.summary: Integrate privacy into technologies, operations, and information architectures in a holistic, integrative, and creative way.
operationalisation: Systemic, principled approach using established standards and frameworks
Allow for external reviews and audits
Apply PDP Principles consistently throughout design and operation
Conduct detailed DPIAs documenting risks and mitigation
Personal data protections that minimise risk and cannot be easily compromised through use, misconfiguration, or error

principles.name: Full functionality – positive sum, not zero sum
principles.summary: Accommodate all legitimate interests in a manner that benefits all stakeholders — privacy can coexist with other legitimate business interests; reject false trade-offs.
operationalisation: Acknowledge that multiple legitimate business interests can coexist
Engage in communication, consultation, and collaboration
Pursue innovative solutions to achieve multiple functionalities
Embed privacy without impairing full functionality
Document and articulate all interests, objectives, desired functions, and metrics

principles.name: End-to-end security – full lifecycle protection
principles.summary: Ensure data security throughout the entire lifecycle: securely retained and securely destroyed at end of process. No gaps in protection or accountability.
operationalisation: TLS for data in transit; AES-256 (or equivalent) for data at rest
Encryption keys managed in a dedicated key management system
Automated, encrypted backups with disaster recovery plan
MFA for staff access; role-based access control with least privilege
Anonymisation and aggregation for analytics where possible
Endpoint protection; staff training on privacy and security
Clear retention policies with secure deletion methods at end of retention
Logging and audit trail with regular review

principles.name: Visibility and transparency – keep it open
principles.summary: Demonstrate accountability for personal data processing. Handle data per stated promises, subject to independent verification.
operationalisation: Accountability: documented privacy-related policies and procedures, assigned to a specified individual; equivalent protection ensured for third-party transfers
Openness: information about data management policies and practices made readily available
Compliance: complaint and redress mechanisms established and clearly communicated
Just-in-time notices when data is collected (e.g., account creation, order placement)
Layered notices: key points emphasised, link to detailed information
Pop-up notices for automatically collected data (cookies, beacons, tracking scripts)
Accessible link to comprehensive notice on every web page

principles.name: Respect for user privacy – keep it user-centric
principles.summary: Keep the interests of the individual paramount through strong privacy defaults, appropriate notice, and empowering user-friendly options.
operationalisation: Consent: free and specific; quality of consent scales with data sensitivity; withdrawable
Accuracy: data accurate, complete, up-to-date as necessary
Access: data subjects can access, challenge accuracy, and amend their data
Compliance: complaint and redress mechanisms with appeal escalation
Privacy dashboard with clear options (toggle data points, customise preferences)
Separate consent for different processing purposes
In-app/timely notifications about policy changes before they take effect
